feedback.text: Hello, I am a citizen of France, concerned about Googles increasing control over the Android ecosystem through commercial lock-in practices. Google recently announced changes that restrict how users can install applications on Android devices. These changes move the system toward a closed "walled garden" model, similar to iOS, where a single gatekeeper acts as both judge and executioner. This reduces consumer freedom, raises switching costs, and consolidates control in the hands of one company. Google has a history of shutting down or restricting projects that threaten its marketplace. These new restrictions appear to target apps such as "Revanced", which offer privacy-focused and ad-free alternatives to YouTube, enabling users to avoid manipulative and predatory business models. By replicating the iOS model, Google strengthens its gatekeeping position in contradiction to the spirit of the Digital Markets Act. At present, EU consumer law does not clearly address ecosystem-level coercion, which creates legal uncertainty and allows companies to test the boundaries until forced to comply. Without stronger measures, fragmentation will continue, as some Member States attempt their own solutions. The Digital Fairness Act should: - Explicitly prevent restrictions on app installation that force consumers into a single marketplace. - Recognise that limiting installation freedom functions as a large-scale dark pattern undermining consumer autonomy. - Complement the DMA by ensuring consumer rights against ecosystem lock-in are clearly defined and enforceable. Strengthening rules in this area would not only protect consumers but also ensure fair competition for EU businesses and increase trust in the digital single market. Open-source alternatives remain essential to guarantee freedom and fairness in the digital environment.
